data.title: DAX Up to New Highs
data.description:
The DAX edged higher toward 22,900 on Wednesday, hitting fresh record highs and extending its winning streak to a third consecutive session.

Investors evaluated new corporate earnings reports and fresh tariff threats while awaiting the latest FOMC minutes for insights into the Federal Reserve's future policy moves.

Meanwhile, attention remained on Ukraine peace negotiations, though meaningful progress is expected to take time.

data.title: Kinder Morgan Completes $640 Million Acquisition of North Dakota Gas System
data.description:
Kinder Morgan said late Tuesday its Hiland Partners Holdings subsidiary completed its $640 million acquisition of a natural gas gathering and processing system in North Dakota from Outrigger Energy II.

The company said the acquired assets include a 270 million cubic-feet per day processing facility and a 104-mile, high-pressure gas gathering pipeline.

Kinder Morgan expects the acquisition to be immediately accretive to shareholders, with a projected 2025 adjusted EBITDA multiple of around 8 times on a full-year basis, it said.

data.title: UK Market Talk Roundup: Shares Losing
data.description:
Stocks on the fall in the UK today. Compiled by Dow Jones Newswires Markets Desk, markets.eu@dowjones.com

0843 GMT - Shares in easyJet and TUI fall, dragged by leisure-travel group peer Jet2's warning that its profit margin could come under pressure. easyJet shares in London trade down 2.9% at 502.60 pence while shares for German travel company TUI are 4% lower at 6.81 euros. Jet2 shares are down 9.65% at 14.14 pounds. Jet2 said that although bookings are up on-year for April, May and June, current macro-economic conditions, pressure on consumer incomes and customers' preference for booking late risk weighing on its performance. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Glencore switching its listing to the U.S. would be a positive catalyst for its shares, Jefferies analysts Christopher LaFemina and Patricia Hove write. The Anglo-Swiss miner said Wednesday that it is reviewing its primary-listing status. Glencore's $1 billion share buyback also helps ease concerns over the slight miss to 2024 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expectations, they write. Meanwhile, Glencore is not expected to benefit from strong commodity tailwinds until next year, they write. Shares trade down 3% at 342.7 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - MTU Aero Engines raised its outlook after delivering earnings which beat expectations, JPMorgan analysts David H. Perry and Lucy Fitzgerald say in a research note. However, the company's cash generation was weaker than expected, which may temper the share price reaction, they say. Fourth-quarter cash generation was 75 million euros below views due to higher-than-expected recall costs on the GTF engine and supply chain challenges, the analysts say. Consensus for cash generation could be trimmed as a result, though earnings consensus for 2025 could rise by a few percentage points, they note. Shares trade 4.7% lower to 330 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

Contact us in London. +44-20-7842-9288
Markettalk.eu@dowjones.com

data.title: UK Market Talk Roundup: Brokers Comments
data.description:
Broker comments in the UK today. Compiled by Dow Jones Newswires Markets Desk, markets.eu@dowjones.com

0843 GMT - Shares in easyJet and TUI fall, dragged by leisure-travel group peer Jet2's warning that its profit margin could come under pressure. easyJet shares in London trade down 2.9% at 502.60 pence while shares for German travel company TUI are 4% lower at 6.81 euros. Jet2 shares are down 9.65% at 14.14 pounds. Jet2 said that although bookings are up on-year for April, May and June, current macro-economic conditions, pressure on consumer incomes and customers' preference for booking late risk weighing on its performance. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Glencore switching its listing to the U.S. would be a positive catalyst for its shares, Jefferies analysts Christopher LaFemina and Patricia Hove write. The Anglo-Swiss miner said Wednesday that it is reviewing its primary-listing status. Glencore's $1 billion share buyback also helps ease concerns over the slight miss to 2024 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expectations, they write. Meanwhile, Glencore is not expected to benefit from strong commodity tailwinds until next year, they write. Shares trade down 3% at 342.7 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0753 GMT - BAE Systems' 2024 results beat market expectations, J.P. Morgan analyst David Perry says. The British aerospace and defense group reported earnings per share 3% ahead of Vuma consensus, while its free cash flow was 890 million pounds above consensus, he says. The company's evolution in 2026 should become clearer in the coming months, once the U.S. administration tables its 2026 defense budget in April, and the U.K. government sets out its military spending plans, Perry notes. "We recommend the shares as a long-term holding; however, after an 11% rally in the last five days we don't expect a major reaction today," he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0733 GMT - The FTSE 100 is seen opening little changed, according to IG. The index closed flat to 8766 points on Tuesday. Data Wednesday showed U.K. inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The Bank of England's "gradual and careful" pace of cutting interest rates looks set to continue for the foreseeable future, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. While more rapid rate cuts are possible in the second half amid stalling economic momentum, stubbornly high inflation won't permit this yet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - HSBC's medium-term guidance for its return on tangible equity will likely be a focus in management's call with analysts and investors, RBC Capital Markets says in a research note after the bank's results. The assumptions underpinning the guidance will prompt questions, analysts Benjamin Toms and Pablo de la Torre Cuevas say. HSBC kept its midterm ROTE guidance unchanged in the mid-teens excluding notable items, while consensus pencils in a 14% return. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0704 GMT - HSBC produced a respectable set of fourth-quarter numbers, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note. The bank's adjusted pretax profit beat consensus on better income and costs, which were partially offset by higher impairments, analysts Elise Yu Ge and Edward Firth say. Its $2 billion share buyback feels light against its recent $3 billion run rate but the dividend it proposed was slightly better, they write. HSBC is the most expensive U.K. bank on a price-to-earnings basis, which is difficult to justify for a group delivering little loan growth and targeting mid-teens returns, the analysts say. "Some may argue that HSBC has traditionally traded at a premium to U.K. banks, but to suggest that Asia/Hong Kong is the same opportunity as it was 15 year ago is obviously flawed," they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

Contact us in London. +44-20-7842-9288
Markettalk.eu@dowjones.com

data.title: Waystar Holding Launches Secondary Public Offering of 18 Million Shares
data.description:
Waystar Holding said late Tuesday that certain selling stockholders are offering 18 million common shares.

The selling stockholders are being offered by certain investment funds of EQT AB, Canada Pension Plan Investment Board, Bain Capital and their affiliates.

The selling stockholders are planning to grant the underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to 2.7 million common shares.

Waystar said it will not receive any proceeds from the offering.

data.title: UK's Jet2 tumbles on rising cost pressures
data.description:
** Jet2 slides 9.3% to 1,420p; biggest loser across London stocks

** British travel company says it continues to experience inflationary input cost pressures exceeding headline CPI rate in hotel accommodations, aircraft maintenances and general airport and Eurocontrol charges

** Expects additional operational costs from aircraft gaps in peak summer on Airbus delivery delays

** Sees 8-10% y/y rise in FY25 profit between 560 mln pounds ($706.9 mln) and 570 mln pounds

** Sale capacity for summer 2025 up 8.5% y/y to 18.6 mln seats

** Jefferies says trading appears robust despite commentary that macro conditions, "demands on consumer discretionary incomes" are driving limited visibility amid continued late bookings

** But, strong trading offset by stronger cost inflation, analysts say

** In 2024, stock rose 26.7%

($1 = 0.7922 pounds)

data.title: Valmet to Deliver New Automation System to Finnish Hydropower Plant
data.description:
Valmet secured an order from KSS Energia for a new hydro turbine automation system for the Verla 2 hydropower plant in Kouvola, Finland.

The Finnish process technologies and automation company will deliver its Valmet DNA hydro turbine automation system to replace an old third-party system at the plant, according to a Wednesday release. No financial details related to the order were disclosed.

The system will be delivered to the customer in December.

data.title: Major Gulf markets subdued on Trump's tariff threats
data.description:
Major stock markets in the Gulf were subdued in early trade on Wednesday, as investors exercised caution following U.S. President Donald Trump's latest tariff threats on auto, semiconductor and pharmaceutical imports.

Since taking office last month, Trump has imposed a 10% tariff on imports from China, and announced plans for 25% tariffs on goods from Mexico and non-energy imports from Canada, although these have been delayed.

Additionally, he has set a date for 25% tariffs on imported steel and aluminium, and is considering reciprocal tariffs on countries that tax U.S. imports.

Saudi Arabia's benchmark index edged 0.1% higher, supported by a 5.5% jump in telecom operator Etihad Etisalat Company (Mobily) , following a significant increase in its annual profit.

In a separate bourse filing, Mobily proposed a higher cash dividend of 1.3 riyals per share for the second half of the year.

SAL Saudi Logistics Services Company tumbled 8.5% to become the top loser in the index, following a decline in its fourth-quarter profit.

The cargo firm also slashed its fourth-quarter cash dividend to 1.33 riyals apiece.

In Abu Dhabi, the index fell 0.3%.

Meanwhile, oil prices edged higher amid worries of oil supply disruptions in the U.S. and Russia, and as markets awaited clarity on the Ukraine peace talks.

Dubai's main share index eased 0.1%, with utility firm Dubai Electricity and Water Authority losing 2.6%.

The Qatari index lost 0.1%, hit by a 0.8% fall in Qatar Navigation .

data.title: Hong Kong Shares Edge 0.1% Lower; Banks Fall — Market Talk
data.description: Hong Kong shares closed slightly lower, with the Hang Seng Index dropping 0.1% to 22944.24. Banks led declines on the index. Hang Seng Bank fell 3.9% after reporting 2024 earnings results and Industrial and Commercial Bank of China was off 1.6%. Chip stocks led gains, with sentiment supported by strong AI demand. Hua Hong Semiconductor jumped 23% and SMIC gained 8.4%. The Hang Seng Tech Index rose 0.6% to 5672.24. China Mengniu Dairy gained 10% after saying it doesn't expect impairment losses to affect its dividend. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0839 GMT - Hong Kong shares closed slightly lower, with the Hang Seng Index dropping 0.1% to 22944.24. Banks led declines on the index. Hang Seng Bank fell 3.9% after reporting 2024 earnings results and Industrial and Commercial Bank of China was off 1.6%. Chip stocks led gains, with sentiment supported by strong AI demand. Hua Hong Semiconductor jumped 23% and SMIC gained 8.4%. The Hang Seng Tech Index rose 0.6% to 5672.24. China Mengniu Dairy gained 10% after saying it doesn't expect impairment losses to affect its dividend. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0829 GMT - European stocks open mixed, with Germany's DAX rising from its latest record closing high and other bourses falling, after U.S. President Trump said he was considering tariffs of 25% or more on automobiles, semiconductors and pharmaceutical products. The Stoxx Europe 600 is down 0.1%, having closed at a record high on Tuesday. Meanwhile, the DAX climbs 0.3% from a record close. The U.K.'s FTSE 100 and France's CAC 40 drop 0.5% and 0.1%, respectively. Italy's FTSE MIB and Spain's Ibex 35--which rose to multiyear highs this week--are up 0.5% and down 0.1%, respectively. There are now many tariff spinning plates in play, but it remains to be seen which of the floated levies will be implemented, strategists at Deutsche bank say in a note. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Glencore switching its listing to the U.S. would be a positive catalyst for its shares, Jefferies analysts Christopher LaFemina and Patricia Hove write. The Anglo-Swiss miner said Wednesday that it is reviewing its primary-listing status. Glencore's $1 billion share buyback also helps ease concerns over the slight miss to 2024 earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation and amortization expectations, they write. Meanwhile, Glencore is not expected to benefit from strong commodity tailwinds until next year, they write. Shares trade down 3% at 342.7 pence. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0824 GMT - MTU Aero Engines raised its outlook after delivering earnings which beat expectations, JPMorgan analysts David H. Perry and Lucy Fitzgerald say in a research note. However, the company's cash generation was weaker than expected, which may temper the share price reaction, they say. Fourth-quarter cash generation was 75 million euros below views due to higher-than-expected recall costs on the GTF engine and supply chain challenges, the analysts say. Consensus for cash generation could be trimmed as a result, though earnings consensus for 2025 could rise by a few percentage points, they note. Shares trade 4.7% lower to 330 euros. (pierre.bertrand@wsj.com)

0823 GMT - BAE Systems reported sales growth across all five segments, with the electronic systems and platforms-and-services units performing particularly well, Matt Dorset, equity analyst at Quilter Cheviot, says. The demand for BAE's products and services is evident, with the order backlog reaching a record high of nearly 80 billion pounds--almost three times its annual sales, he says. The British aerospace and defense manufacturer has substantial exposure to European demand, just as the continent, including the U.K., prepares to step up defense spending, Dorset says. "We continue to see structural tailwinds supporting BAE, driven by increased global defense spending," he writes. Shares are up 0.5% at 13.44 pounds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - ASR Nederland didn't provide an update on the almost 30% stake that is owned by Aegon and resulted from ASR's purchase of its peer's Dutch insurance business in 2023 but posted solid results, J.P. Morgan says in a research note after the Dutch insurer's annual print. "We would view any move by Aegon to start reducing its stake, accompanied by additional neutralising buybacks from ASR, as potential positive catalyst--but the timing remains uncertain," they say. The shares should react positively to the solid set of results and lift consensus estimates, analysts write. ASR rises 3.75% to 50.58 euros while Aegon is up 1.2% at 6.48 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0805 GMT - Singapore's grocers and malls are set to be the biggest beneficiaries of generous handouts from the country's budget, say DBS Group Research analysts in a note. A set of support measures was announced to help households and individuals tackle rising cost-of-living pressures. Various vouchers will support retail-related spending, particularly on essentials at suburban retail malls, DBS says. Among other beneficiaries are supermarkets and food-court operators, the bank adds. Sheng Siong Group is best positioned to gain as a pure domestic grocery retailer, DBS says. (amanda.lee@wsj.com)

0801 GMT - Julius Baer has a relatively narrow range of options to implement its new strategy, prompting Jefferies to downgrade its rating on the stock to hold from buy, the broker says in a research note. "New CEO, Stefan Bollinger, deserves an open-minded assessment of his strategic vision for the firm...but we prefer to approach it from a neutral stance," analysts write. The initial comments by the Swiss bank's new boss suggest his outlook will be based on a client focus, technology, disciplined entrepreneurship, its performance and ownership-led culture and ambition to be a prime spot for talent, they say. These seem "perfectly reasonable maxims" but cost actions might still be needed to bring its cost-to-income ratio down. The share price now reflects its incremental cost-efficiency measures and lack of near-term share buyback, they write. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0753 GMT - BAE Systems' 2024 results beat market expectations, J.P. Morgan analyst David Perry says. The British aerospace and defense group reported earnings per share 3% ahead of Vuma consensus, while its free cash flow was 890 million pounds above consensus, he says. The company's evolution in 2026 should become clearer in the coming months, once the U.S. administration tables its 2026 defense budget in April, and the U.K. government sets out its military spending plans, Perry notes. "We recommend the shares as a long-term holding; however, after an 11% rally in the last five days we don't expect a major reaction today," he adds. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0737 GMT - Germany's DAX index could come under pressure today after President Trump spoke about new tariffs on cars, pharmaceuticals and semiconductors, IG says. "With new statements from Trump about tariffs, the DAX could possibly be the big loser of the day, as cars and pharmaceuticals are a large part of the index." However, it's unclear when these tariffs would be implemented and more information will come on April 2, IG says. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0733 GMT - The FTSE 100 is seen opening little changed, according to IG. The index closed flat to 8766 points on Tuesday. Data Wednesday showed U.K. inflation increased to 3.0% year-on-year in January from 2.5% in December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected 2.8%. Core inflation rose to 3.7% from 3.2% a month ago, as expected. The Bank of England's "gradual and careful" pace of cutting interest rates looks set to continue for the foreseeable future, Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. While more rapid rate cuts are possible in the second half amid stalling economic momentum, stubbornly high inflation won't permit this yet. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0727 GMT - Inflation rose faster than expected at the start of the year but the Bank of England is unlikely to be deterred from its plans for gradual rate-cuts this year, writes Joe Nellis, economic adviser to consultancy MHA. Consumer prices rose 3% on year in January, picking up more pace than economists had estimated, figures show Wednesday. But that acceleration was down to regulated prices--such as energy and value-added taxes--rather than underlying pressures, Nellis notes. "A close watch will be kept on price rises for some months ahead, but the Bank will be content knowing that sustainable inflationary pressures remain cooled," he tells clients. "At least for now."(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: Paltac's Attributable Profit Rises Nearly 7% in Fiscal Nine Months
data.description:
Paltac's profit attributable to owners of the parent rose 6.6% to 18.3 billion yen in the first nine months ended Dec. 31, from 17.1 billion yen a year ago.

The wholesaler of cosmetic and OTC drugs' basic earnings per share increased to 292.09 yen from 272.80 yen a year ago, according to a delayed Tokyo Stock Exchange filing.

Net sales grew 3.2% to 911.1 billion yen from more than 883 billion yen a year earlier.

It forecasted attributable profit to grow 6.6% to 22 billion yen, or 350.06 yen per share, and net sales to gain 3.3% to 1.19 trillion yen for the fiscal year ending March 31.

It declared an interim dividend of 50 yen per share, paid on Dec. 2, 2024, and expects to pay a year-end dividend of 55 yen per share, bringing the full-year dividend to 105 yen per share.

data.title: Soccer-Shandong withdraw from Asian Champions League before Ulsan fixture
data.description:
Chinese Super League side Shandong Taishan have pulled out of the Asian Champions League Elite just hours before their final league phase game against Ulsan HD in South Korea on Wednesday.

The Asian Football Confederation (AFC) said in a statement that Shandong were "considered to have withdrawn" from the competition after the club confirmed they did not intend to report for the game at Ulsan Munsu Football Stadium.

No reason was given for Shandong's failure to travel for the game and the regional body said the issue would be referred to the relevant AFC committees.

Shandong took 10 points from their first seven games and needed only a draw against Ulsan to confirm their progress to the last 16.

Their withdrawal means fellow Chinese side Shanghai Shenhua are guaranteed a place in the next round.

data.title: Update: Market Chatter: Honda Motor Open to Reviving Merger Talks with Nissan if CEO Uchida Steps Down
data.description:
(Updates with comments from Honda and Nissan in the sixth paragraph.)

Honda Motor is considering resuming takeover talks with Nissan to become the world's fourth-largest carmaker if Nissan CEO Makoto Uchida steps down, the Financial Times reported Monday, citing sources familiar with the discussions.

The report said Uchida, a key advocate for the merger, has faced deteriorating relations with Honda's CEO Toshihiro Mibe, particularly over the slow pace of Nissan's restructuring and its financial struggles.

The merger talks collapsed last week, where both companies, in a joint statement, cited concerns over various possible structures of a combined company.

Uchida, who had hoped to remain in his position until 2026, is under increasing pressure to step down following failed negotiations for a megadeal, according to the Financial Times.

Nissan's board had also begun informal discussions over the timing of his exit, the report said, citing a person familiar with those talks.

Honda did not provide any specific comment on resuming mergers talks, while Nissan declined to comment.

(Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.)

data.title: European Stocks Little Changed
data.description:
Both the STOXX 50 and STOXX 600 hovered near the flatline on Wednesday after closing at record highs in the previous session.

Traders focused on fresh corporate earnings and rising trade tensions while monitoring the situation in Ukraine.

HSBC fell 1.6% after its full-year earnings and revenue disappointed, while Glencore tumbled 4.4% following a decline in full-year profit.

Shares of Philips also dropped more than 6% after the company missed fourth-quarter sales growth expectations.

Meanwhile, US President Donald Trump indicated he would likely impose 25% tariffs on automobile, semiconductor, and pharmaceutical imports, with an announcement expected as soon as April 2nd.

data.title: Gilt Yields Rise to 3-Week Highs After Above-Forecast U.K. Inflation — Market Talk
data.description: Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, rise as prospects of further near-term interest-rate cuts by the Bank of England decline following stronger-than-expected U.K. inflation data. Annual CPI inflation rose to 3.0% in January, from 2.5% in December. "This [data] highlights the challenges the BOE continues to face as it aims to get back to 2% inflation," Moneyfarm's Richard Flax says in a note. The 10-year gilt yield climbs 4 basis points to 4.601%, Tradeweb data show. The 2-year gilt yield rises 3 basis points to 4.270%. Both trade at their highest in three weeks. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)
